$comment: Needfire emergency protocols. Hand-derived from the seed corpus documents named in source_doc — every step must be traceable to its source. Schema: Protocol{id,title,short,icon,severity(life-threat|urgent|serious),keywords[],source_doc,entry_warning?,disclaimer,start,steps[]}; Step{id,type(action|decision|loop|info),text(markdown),detail?,warning?,timer?,options?(decision only, 2-4),next?(id|END|omitted=next in array),loop{until,back_to}?}; Timer{kind(metronome|countdown|stamp),label,bpm?,seconds?,count?,persist?,auto?(never true for metronome)}.

protocols.title: CPR — Adult, Child, Infant
protocols.entry_warning: Send someone for help and an AED NOW.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Check response.** Shout and tap the shoulders. Look for normal breathing — no more than 10 seconds. **Gasping is NOT normal breathing.**
**Call it: what are you seeing?**
They do not need CPR. Lay them on their **side** (recovery position), keep the airway clear, keep them warm, and **watch their breathing continuously**.
**Who is the patient?** This changes hand position and depth.
**If you are alone:** go get help and an AED NOW, then come back and start compressions. If someone else is here, they go — you start compressions immediately.
If you are alone with a child or infant, **do CPR for about 2 minutes BEFORE leaving them** to get help.

**Compress the centre of the chest, hard and fast.** Firm surface. Rate **100–120/min**. Let the chest recoil fully; minimise pauses.

- **Adult:** two hands, 5–6 cm (2 in) deep
- **Child:** one or two hands, about 5 cm
- **Infant:** two fingers (or two thumbs, hands encircling), about 4 cm
steps.detail: Push with straight arms, shoulders over hands, using your body weight. Cracked ribs happen — keep going.

steps.text: **Are you trained in rescue breaths?** Breaths matter more for children, infants, and drowning victims.
**30 compressions, then 2 breaths.** Tilt head, lift chin. Cover an infant's mouth AND nose. The metronome counter rolls over at 30.

loop.until: Signs of life, an AED tells you to stand clear, trained help takes over, or you physically cannot continue. Swap rescuers every 2 minutes.

steps.text: **Continuous compressions.** Do not stop to check. Swap rescuers every 2 minutes if you can.

loop.until: Signs of life, an AED tells you to stand clear, trained help takes over, or you physically cannot continue.

steps.text: **When an AED arrives:** attach it immediately, turn it on, follow its voice prompts. **Resume compressions immediately after any shock.**
steps.warning: Do not stop CPR to wait for the AED — keep compressing until the pads are on and the machine says stand clear.

protocols.title: Severe Bleeding
protocols.entry_warning: Severe bleeding can be fatal in minutes. Act immediately.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Apply firm direct pressure** on the wound with a clean cloth or your hand. Press HARD and do not release to "check" — keep pressure constant.
steps.detail: Protect yourself from blood contact if possible (gloves, a barrier), but do not delay for a serious arterial bleed.

steps.text: **Deep wound? Pack it.** Pack gauze or clean cloth firmly INTO the wound, then keep pressing on top.
**Is the bleeding slowing after several minutes of hard, constant pressure?**
**Keep maximum direct pressure.** For wounds where a tourniquet can't go (neck, torso, groin), packing + relentless pressure is the treatment.

loop.until: Bleeding controlled or trained help takes over.

steps.text: **Apply a tourniquet** 5–7 cm (2–3 in) ABOVE the wound — not on a joint. Tighten until the bleeding STOPS. It should hurt; that means it's working.
steps.warning: Once on, do NOT loosen or remove it. Note the time.

steps.text: **Do not remove the soaked dressing** — add more on top. Bandage firmly to hold the pressure. Keep the wound covered and the person still.

**Watch for shock:** pale, cold, clammy skin; rapid weak pulse; rapid breathing; confusion; thirst. Lay them flat, raise the legs if no spinal or leg injury, keep them warm, keep monitoring. Seek the highest level of care available.

protocols.title: Choking / Blocked Airway
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Can they cough, speak, or cry?**

options.label: No — silent, can't breathe, turning blue

steps.text: **Encourage them to keep coughing.** A strong cough beats anything you can do. Do NOT slap the back of someone who is coughing effectively. Stay with them.

loop.until: The object comes out, or they stop being able to cough/speak (then go back and choose the silent option).

steps.text: **Adult/child, or infant under 1 year?**
**5 back blows.** Lean them forward. Strike hard between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand. Check the mouth after each blow.
steps.detail: If pregnant or too large to reach around the abdomen: use CHEST thrusts instead of abdominal thrusts.

steps.text: **5 abdominal thrusts.** Stand behind them, fist just above the navel, grasp with the other hand, pull sharply inward and UPWARD. Alternate: 5 back blows, 5 thrusts.
steps.warning: Never do a blind finger sweep — you can push the object deeper.

loop.until: The object comes out, or they go unconscious.

steps.text: **Infant: 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts.** Lay the infant face-down along your forearm, head low; give 5 firm back blows. Turn face-up; give 5 chest thrusts with two fingers on the breastbone. NO abdominal thrusts on infants.

loop.until: The object comes out, or the infant goes limp/unconscious.

steps.text: **Unconscious → start CPR now.** Lower them gently to the ground. Before each set of rescue breaths, LOOK in the mouth and remove the object only if you can SEE it.
Open the **CPR protocol** and follow it: [CPR — Adult, Child, Infant](#/emergency/cpr)

protocols.title: Burns — Heat, Chemical, Electrical
protocols.entry_warning: Stop the burning first — get them away from the source.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Stop the burning.** Remove the person from the source. Smother flames — stop, drop, roll.
**What kind of burn?**
**Brush off any dry powder first, then flush with water for 20+ minutes.** Remove contaminated clothing; protect yourself.

**Cut the power BEFORE touching the casualty.** Electrical damage runs deep between entry and exit wounds even when the skin looks minor. **Watch for heart rhythm problems** — if they collapse or stop breathing normally, go to [CPR](#/emergency/cpr).
**Cool the burn with cool running water for 20 minutes.** Cooling helps even if started an hour after the burn.
steps.warning: No ice, no ice water — ice deepens the injury.

steps.text: **Remove rings, watches, and tight clothing early**, before swelling starts. Do not pull off clothing stuck to the burn.
**Cover with a clean non-stick dressing** or kitchen cling film laid loosely over the wound. Keep the person warm — large burns lose heat fast.
steps.warning: Do NOT pop blisters, or apply butter, toothpaste, oil, or grease — they trap heat and cause infection.

steps.text:
**Check for a critical burn.** The victim's own palm (with fingers) is about 1% of their body surface — count palms to estimate size. Any of these present?

- Burns to the **face, hands, genitals**, or over joints
- **Airway burn** signs: singed nostrils, hoarse voice, soot in mouth
- More than **10% of body surface**
- **Painless, white, leathery, or charred** areas (full-thickness)
- Smoke inhalation

**Treat as life-threatening — evacuate to the highest care available.** While waiting: keep the wound covered, keep them warm, and give frequent sips of water or oral rehydration solution if they are awake and able to drink — large burns leak enormous amounts of fluid and cause shock.

Keep the dressing clean and the person warm. For any **large burn**, give frequent sips of water or oral rehydration solution to an awake person who can drink — large burns leak fluid and cause shock.

protocols.title: Anaphylaxis — Severe Allergic Reaction
protocols.entry_warning: Epinephrine first and fast — the dangerous mistake is giving it late.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text:
**Give epinephrine (adrenaline) NOW** into the muscle of the **outer mid-thigh**, through clothing if needed.

- **Adult: 0.3 mg** auto-injector
- **Child: 0.15 mg** (roughly under 25–30 kg / 55–66 lb)
steps.detail: Suspect anaphylaxis when, soon after a sting, food, or medicine: swelling of the lips, tongue, or throat; hoarse voice or wheeze; widespread hives or flushing; vomiting or cramps; dizziness or collapse. Epinephrine given early is very safe.
steps.warning: Antihistamines are NOT a substitute for epinephrine. They may ease itching but do nothing for the airway or blood pressure — never delay epinephrine to try them.

steps.text: **Position them — how are they?**
steps.warning: Do NOT stand the person up suddenly — a sudden upright move can cause fatal collapse when blood pressure is low.

steps.text: Let them **sit up** to ease breathing. Keep watching them.
Put them in the **recovery position** (on their side) so the airway stays clear.
Lay her on her **left side**.
**Lie them flat and raise the legs.**
**Repeat the dose after 5–15 minutes if they are not clearly improving.** Keep a second epinephrine dose within reach.

timer.label: Reassess — repeat dose if not improving

steps.text: **Watch them closely for at least 4–6 hours** — symptoms can return hours later with no new exposure (a biphasic reaction). Watch longer after a severe reaction. Remove any remaining stinger by scraping it out. **Evacuate to medical care even if they recover fully.**

protocols.title: Poisoning
protocols.entry_warning: Do NOT induce vomiting — for any poison.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **How did the poison get in?**
**Rinse the mouth with water and spit.** Give nothing further by mouth — no milk, no salt water, no charcoal — unless a trusted reference for that specific substance says otherwise.
steps.warning: Do NOT induce vomiting. Vomiting corrosives burns the throat twice; vomiting fuels floods the lungs.

steps.text: **Remove contaminated clothing** (protect your own hands) and **flush the skin with water for 15–20 minutes**.
**Flush the eyes for 15–20 minutes** with lukewarm clean water, eyelids held open, water flowing from the nose side outward.
**Protect yourself FIRST — do not become the second casualty** in a fume-filled space. Get the person to fresh air.
steps.detail: Carbon monoxide from stoves, heaters, engines, or charcoal in an enclosed shelter is invisible and odorless — headache, nausea, dizziness, confusion. A headache shared by everyone in the shelter is CO until proven otherwise: get everyone out into fresh air and ventilate before returning.

steps.text: **Identify what happened**: the substance, the container and its label, how much, and when. **Keep the container.**
**Check them — responsive and breathing?**
Place them in the **recovery position** and monitor the airway continuously.
**Start CPR now.** Open the CPR protocol and follow it: [CPR](#/emergency/cpr)

**Keep watching — some poisons act late.** The deadliest wild mushrooms cause symptoms **delayed 6+ hours** (late onset is a bad sign, not a reassuring one). Botulism from badly canned food: blurred vision, drooping lids, descending weakness. An aspirated mouthful of fuel can inflame the lungs — watch breathing for 24 hours.

protocols.title: Snakebite
protocols.entry_warning: Move away from the snake. Treat every bite as venomous.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Keep the victim calm and still.** Have them sit or lie down; carry them if possible rather than letting them walk. Panic and exertion pump venom through the body faster — your first medicine is calm.
**Immobilize the bitten limb** with a splint or sling and keep it at about **heart level**.
**Remove rings, watches, and tight clothing NOW**, before swelling makes it impossible.
**Mark the edge of the swelling** with a pen and write the time next to it. Repeat every 15–30 minutes to track spread.
**Wash the bite gently** and cover with a clean dressing.
steps.warning: Do NOT cut the wound, suck out venom, apply a tourniquet, apply ice, or give alcohol. All of these worsen the injury or speed the venom, and none remove it.

steps.text: **Is the snake a known NEUROTOXIC type** — an Australian elapid, coral snake, or similar?
**Apply a pressure-immobilization bandage**: a firm wrap of the whole limb (snug as for a sprain) plus a splint. This slows paralysing venom.
steps.warning: Do NOT use this for vipers or rattlesnakes — their venom destroys tissue, and trapping it in the limb concentrates that damage.

steps.text: **Antivenom is the only definitive treatment — evacuate to medical care**, even if symptoms seem mild; envenomation can worsen over hours. Note the snake's colour, pattern, and size **from a safe distance** (a photo is ideal); do not try to catch or kill it. Some bites inject no venom ("dry bites"), but **never assume you got one**.

protocols.title: Seizure
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Clear hard and sharp objects away** from the person. Most seizures stop on their own within 1–3 minutes — your job is to protect them from injury while it runs its course.
**Cushion the head** with something soft — a folded jacket, your hands. Loosen anything tight around the neck. **Do not restrain** the movements.
steps.warning: Put NOTHING in the mouth. They cannot swallow their tongue; forcing objects in breaks teeth and blocks the airway.

steps.text: **Time the seizure from the moment it starts.**

timer.label: Seizure duration — over 5 min is an emergency

steps.text: **When the shaking stops, roll them into the recovery position** (on their side, head tilted slightly back) so saliva or vomit drains. Confusion and drowsiness afterward are normal. Stay with them until they are fully awake and speak calmly.

**Any of these emergency signs?**

- Seizure lasting **more than 5 minutes**
- Seizures **back-to-back** without waking in between
- The person's **first-ever seizure**
- It happened **in water**, caused **injury**, or the person is **pregnant**
- They do **not wake** within minutes of the shaking stopping
**Do everything you can to reach help.** A seizure lasting more than 5 minutes (status epilepticus) may not stop on its own and is life-threatening.

**Think about the cause:** high fever in a child (cool gradually — remove layers, lukewarm cloth; do not plunge into cold water); a head injury in the last days; drinking only plain water without salt after heavy sweating (rehydrate with ORS, not plain water alone); alcohol or medication withdrawal; low blood sugar — **if they wake fully, give sugar**.

protocols.title: Drowning
protocols.entry_warning: Rescue from shore — do not become the second victim.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Reach, throw, row — don't go.** Reach with a stick, paddle, or clothing; throw a rope or anything that floats; row a boat to them. Enter the water only as a last resort, and bring something that floats.
steps.warning: A drowning person will climb on you and pull you under.

steps.text: **Once out of the water, check breathing** — no more than 10 seconds. **Gasping is not breathing.**
**Are they breathing normally?**

**Give 5 initial rescue breaths BEFORE starting compressions.** Tilt head, lift chin, seal your mouth over theirs — mouth AND nose for a small child. Drowning arrest comes from lack of oxygen, so ventilation comes first — the opposite of typical adult cardiac arrest.
**Then continue CPR at 30 compressions to 2 breaths.** Open the CPR protocol and follow it: [CPR](#/emergency/cpr)
steps.warning: Do NOT waste time trying to "drain water" from the lungs with abdominal thrusts or by hanging them head-down. It does not work and delays CPR.

steps.text: **Expect vomiting** — it happens in most drowning resuscitations. **Roll them to the side, clear the mouth, and continue.**

**Handle gently — cold water victims are often hypothermic too.** If they are breathing but not fully awake, put them in the **recovery position** (on their side) and keep checking breathing. Remove wet clothing, insulate from the ground, and warm gradually. Do not give up resuscitation early on a cold victim — **"not dead until warm and dead."**

**Watch them for 24 hours.** Anyone who inhaled water can deteriorate over the next 24 hours — worsening cough, fast or laboured breathing, unusual tiredness. **Treat new breathing trouble as an emergency.**

protocols.title: Heat Exhaustion and Heat Stroke
protocols.entry_warning: The dividing line is the brain: confusion means heat stroke.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **What are you seeing?** Heat illness is a spectrum: cramps → exhaustion → stroke.

options.label: Confused, strange behaviour, stumbling, seizure, or collapse — hot skin (dry OR still sweaty)
Heavy sweating, weak, nauseous, dizzy, pale clammy skin — but thinking clearly

steps.text: **HEAT STROKE — cool aggressively NOW. Cool first, transport second.** Whole-body immersion in cool water (stream, trough, tub) is the best treatment — keep the head supported above water.
**No immersion available?** Douse continuously with water and fan hard, and put cold packs at the **neck, armpits, and groin**.

**Stop active cooling once they become alert.** Give nothing by mouth while confused. Then get them to advanced medical care even if they seem recovered — heat stroke injures organs after the crisis passes.
steps.warning: Shivering alone is NOT a reason to stop — heat stroke victims often shiver while still dangerously hot.

steps.text: **Stop all work.** Move to shade and loosen or remove clothing.
**Lie them flat with legs raised.**
**Sip water or oral rehydration solution steadily.** Cool with a wet cloth on the skin plus fanning. Recovery should come within 30 minutes.
**Recovering within 30 minutes?**

options.label: No — or the mind is clouding

steps.text: **Keep them at rest in the shade** and continue sips of fluid. Before returning to work: schedule hard work for dawn and dusk, and drink about 0.5–1 litre per hour **with electrolytes or salted food** — plain water alone for hours risks dangerously low sodium.

protocols.title: Hypothermia and Frostbite
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Whole body, or a body part?**

options.label: Whole body: shivering, clumsy, slurred speech, confused
A frozen part: skin hard, waxy, wooden
Skin numb and white but still SOFT (frostnip)
Wet, cold feet: numb, pale, swollen — but not frozen (trench foot)

steps.text: **Frostnip — rewarm it skin-to-skin**: fingers in an armpit, a warm palm on a cheek. No harm done.

**Get them into shelter, out of the wind, and off the cold ground** — ground contact steals body heat, so insulate underneath at least as much as above. Remove wet clothing; insulate with dry layers and cover the head and neck.
**Apply gentle warmth to the core** — chest, neck, armpits, groin. Give warm sweet drinks **only if fully alert** and able to swallow. **No alcohol.**
steps.warning: Do not rub the limbs or apply sudden intense heat — harmful in severe cases. Handle a severely hypothermic person gently.

steps.text: **Loss of shivering is a danger sign, not improvement.** Worsening hypothermia: shivering stops, confusion, drowsiness, slow weak pulse. Keep handling gently and seek advanced care.
**Get out of the cold.** Remove wet clothing, and remove **rings and watches** before swelling. Protect the frozen part from bumps and pressure.
**Could the part refreeze after thawing?** Choose the moment of rewarming deliberately.
**Do NOT rewarm yet** — refreezing destroys tissue far worse than staying frozen a few hours longer. **Walking on frozen feet to reach shelter is acceptable; walking on thawed feet is not.**

loop.until: You are in shelter and refreezing is impossible — then rewarm.

steps.text: **Immerse the part in warm water at 37–39°C (99–102°F)** — comfortable to your own elbow, never hot. Keep it there **20–30 minutes**, topping up warm water, until the tissue is soft and red or purple. Severe pain during rewarming is expected — a sign of returning circulation. Give pain relief if available.
steps.warning: NEVER rub the part with snow, massage it, or hold it to direct dry heat — a fire or stove will burn tissue that cannot feel.

steps.text: **Leave blisters intact.** Place dry gauze between fingers and toes, bandage loosely, and elevate. **Do not let the part refreeze** — do not walk on thawed feet.

**Trench foot — wet and cold above freezing for hours to days.** Get the feet dry: remove wet boots and socks, air and gently dry the feet, and keep them clean. **Rewarm gradually** — body warmth or a warm room, not hot water. Elevate the feet and avoid walking on them where possible. Change into dry socks daily to prevent it coming back.
steps.warning: Do not rub the feet, and do not use hot water or direct dry heat — the tissue is fragile.

protocols.title: Emergency Childbirth
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Wash your hands thoroughly** and lay out clean cloths or plastic under the mother. Most births proceed normally with little intervention — your job is cleanliness, warmth, and calm support.
**Let labor progress at its own pace.** Do not push on the abdomen or tell her to push before she feels the urge.

**As the head crowns, support it gently with a clean hand** so it emerges slowly. **Do not pull on the head or body.** Check for the cord around the neck — if present, slip it gently over the head. The shoulders and body usually follow within a contraction or two; the baby will be slippery — hold securely.
**Dry the baby vigorously** and place it **skin-to-skin on the mother's chest**, covered with dry cloth. Warmth is critical.
**Is the baby crying and breathing?** Most do within a minute.

**Rub the back firmly and flick the soles of the feet.** If the baby still does not breathe after about a minute of drying and stimulation, **start gentle rescue breaths**: cover the baby's mouth AND nose with your mouth and give small puffs — see the infant guidance in [CPR](#/emergency/cpr). Do not give up quickly.
**Do not cut the cord early.** Wait until it stops pulsing, then tie it tightly in two places with clean cord (about 5 and 10 cm from the baby) and cut between the ties with a clean blade.
**The placenta usually delivers within 30 minutes.** If it has not delivered within 30–60 minutes, **treat it as a danger sign** — retained placenta causes severe bleeding.

**After the placenta delivers, massage the mother's lower abdomen firmly** until the uterus feels like a hard ball — this limits bleeding. Put the baby to the breast; nursing helps the uterus contract.
**Danger signs — seek skilled help:** heavy bleeding, feet or cord appearing first, or labor lasting many hours without progress. These are emergencies beyond home care.
